Okay, so now you come back here to this slide. So this is kind of showing, I'm going to keep building on this slide each time. So now we've got the three core things in the plot line. Here's the attractive character and here's how we build a port. Like I said, the first 10% of every single movie, so he was saying like on page, whatever it was, 22 in the screenplay is always the first 10% and that's when the hero leaves. Like physically leaves where they're at. Frodo leaves the Shire, right? Lightning McQueen leaves the racetrack. Belle leaves her house. It's always like the character is leaving. So the initial, they're there in this environment where you're understanding the character, you're building a port with them and then the character actually physically leaves. There's a lot of my videos, if you're noticing like, I'm not just standing at my desk, I'm physically leaving like, hey, I'm getting in my car, come on guys, let's go. And I jump and I'm leaving because I'm trying to take somebody with me on a journey. Like I'm physically leaving. Frank Kern's great at this too. He always watches the videos. He's never there in front of the desk. He's like, hey guys, how's it going? Come on over here and he's leaving and bringing people on a journey with them. I was talking to Dan during the break that there's a video I want to make here where like, someone opens the door and they're coming to ClickFunnels and I welcome them to ClickFunnels and I take them and we walk to a different place because as soon as they're doing that, they're following you on this journey. So not only am I doing it like as I'm speaking, but like a lot of times physically in the video or however you're using it, I'm leaving on this journey, right? Now what's happening is that, again, this is called the hero's two journeys. The first journey is what we call the journey of achievement. This is what the hero's setting out to. They want to do this thing, right? And that's what they're always thinking about. Like I got to go win this race and I find that this thing's happening. So this is like what's very known to the audience. The audience knows like Frodo's leaving because he's going to take the ring and he's going to leave the shy. He's coming over here. He's got to drop it into the volcano and destroy the ring. Like that's the journey of achievement. I got to achieve this end goal to get my desires, right? And then the second journey down here is called the journey of transformation. This is the journey that we're not aware of by watching the outside. This is what's happening where the character actually is transforming as a person. Okay, so the two journeys, journey of achievement and the journey of transformation. Okay? Now if you come back here, this is the journey of transformation, the second journey. What's happening is during the backstory of the character, you're learning about their identity. Like this is who I am. This is who I believe that I am. Like I believe these things are important to me. All this stuff's happening, right? Think about any movie. Like these are the things that are important to me. And then as they leave and they go on this journey, all this conflict starts happening and all their beliefs, like the things that they hold true themselves, like the identity they have, they start realizing that it's not good or it's wrong or something. And they go through this journey. All these things start falling apart. Eventually at the end of it, the journey of transformation happens and they become a new person. They have a new belief system because of this journey, right? So if you look at this from the hero's two journeys, this journey right here, journey of achievement, down here is the journey of transformation where you're leaving your old identity. So I'm going to kind of tell you guys a story from a movie and I'm going to plug it into this framework so you can kind of see the whole thing. Okay? I'm going to share it with the movie from movie Cars. Have you guys seen Cars like 50 times? So good. So Lightning McQueen is the character. He has a desire to win the Piston Cup. He runs into conflict when he gets stuck in radiator springs, right? Boom. There's a storyline. So the first 10% of the movie, we are at a certain location where the character feels safe, feels very, very good. Things are happening and we're introduced to him, right? So start going through and things are happening. We see he's powerful, right? He's kind of funny. He's doing things like that, right? And we're building this rapport with him. You're seeing him racing to the first rookie ever to win the Piston Cup. It's all exciting. You have this whole backstory. And then he loses it or they tie, right? They three-way tie and it ends. And so because that, he has to go on this journey. If he wants to achieve his thing, he wants to win the Piston Cup, I got to go on this journey. I have to go retrieve the Piston Cup. I have to go win it. I have to leave, right? So he gets in Mac, gets in the car and we have this really cool glimpse of Lightning McQueen that makes us actually care about him. Because the first is like this cool, powerful guy, super awesome. They get in the car and then his agent gets on the phone and he starts talking to his agent. And basically lets in this little glimpse and we know that Lightning McQueen is actually super insecure and nobody really likes him. And that's why he's so powerful. But he has no friends. He's like, hey, I got tickets for your friends. Who do you want me to give them to? And he's like, oh, you're going to be the right one. Well, I can't actually make it, but I'll be watching your band. And we're always like, wow, he's actually vulnerable. We start seeing the character flaws in Lightning McQueen. Like, wow, this thing happens, right? So then 10% of the movie, now we actually care about this character. We've got some relationship with him. And then he leaves because he's got to go on this journey of achievement. He's leaving and all of a sudden conflict happens, right? And he gets stuck in radiator springs. He's locked up and he's going through all these things and he's trying to win. He's trying to get to the thing. He's got to get to your win piston cup over and over and over again. And all this conflict is happening thing after thing after thing and all this conflict is happening. And throughout the entire movie, you can see all this conflict happening over and over. Every time he tries to leave, he gets caught. This thing happens. This thing happens. And at the end of it, finally, he gets to go to the race. He shows up the piston cup. He goes out there and he's racing and he's kind of miserable. He's not having them. He's doing well, but he's not really that happy. And then Doc and all those guys come and they become the sideline, right? So he goes out there and he's doing this whole race. And he's getting a point where he actually has a shot. A shot to actually win this thing that he's been trying to get his whole entire life. And there's a point like he crashes and there's no way he can actually win. He comes back and he goes back through it again and finds the point. He's about to win. He's racing and he comes down to the finish line and he's just screaming through. He's beating everybody. He comes to the finish line, looks up to the monitor and he notices something just happened. He kicks, who's like the jerk of the whole thing. He hits the king who he's racing against, right? Hits the king, the king's car flips out of control, gets completely destroyed, smashed up into little pieces. He looks up to the monitor and he sees the king smashed on the side. He realizes he's not going to finish this race. And all of a sudden, his achievement becomes in jeopardy. The cool thing about the hero's two journeys, sometimes the hero achieves their thing. Sometimes they don't. What's interesting, we don't really care if they achieve this. What's more interesting is the transformation that happens to the character down here. So what happens? Lightning McQueen sees that. He slams on his brakes. It comes all the way to finish line, stops. And Chick Hicks flies past him, whoom, wins the race. Lightning McQueen backs up, comes over to the king, gets behind him and starts pushing him to the finish line. And the king says, dude, what are you doing? He's like, you just lost yourself to Piston Cup. And then Lightning McQueen says, oh, you know, a old friend of mine once told me, you know, it's just an empty cup. It's not that big a deal. And he pushes the king across, and we have a chance to see that not only he misses achievement, but he transformed as a person. That's the real journey. That's the journey we actually cared about was the journey of transformation. Isn't that awesome? I have wheels. Yeah. I was going through this thing a little while ago, and I mapped it out, and I was trying to figure out movies, and I thought about that one. I was just like, God, how cool is that? We're always trying to achieve this thing, achieve this thing, achieve this thing. We think that's what people actually care about, but it's not. We didn't care if Lightning McQueen hit it or not. We cared about that. Him taking this insecure person with no friends and through the story, he came something different, became someone more. That's what we care about. That's why we love that movie. That's why I watch 30 times with my kids, because of that. That's the cool thing. It's the second journey. So that's kind of the framework of most movies. So I want you guys, I want you guys to think back through one of the last movies you saw, which I want you to remember the best. What made you actually care about that person? And I'm guessing for most of you guys, they physically left that location. It's really interesting. They physically left that location. She goes on this journey and she realizes that people can and will help her and that the mother was the one who was trying to keep her alone and she eventually escapes and sees that she's actually the princess. I love that movie. So much better than Frozen. One more. One more first-match share. Frozen got all big accolades and everyone cares. That movie sucked. It was like the best ever. I had to crush on Rapunzel. I'm not going to lie. Just kidding. Okay. So the last movie I watched was called Lion. It's in theaters now. I don't know. But it's about this little boy who's five years old. He is living in the streets of India as like a poor Indian child. And him and his brother, he always wants to help his brother out, his older brother. And so he begs his older brother to take him to go, I guess, collect money. You're kind of understanding that he's going to go handle or something like that. So he's like, okay, you can come with me, but you have to stay right here on this train bench. So they ride the train together. Sorry, they ride the train together, get to the next station. And he's like, you have to stay right here. Don't move. Well, he moves, gets on a train, goes like 2,000 miles away from home. He's five years old, wanders the streets trying to figure out what can I do as a child, right? He doesn't know anything. And it's showing this like, you're like really emotionally engaging in this suffering of this child. And he ends up getting picked up by like six traffickers. And he like gets away from them. And then this guy like finds him and he takes him to an orphanage and he gets adopted by parents from Australia. So then he goes even further from home in Australia. And he grows and then it flashes back to show him or flashes forward to show him as an adult. And he keeps having these flashbacks of being a little child at home. And he's like, I need to get home. I need to get home. Who are my parents? Who's my family? I don't know who they are. But if I can just find, if I can just find that station, that train station, I think I could get back home. And so he maps out the whole, all the train stations in India, which were a lot. And finally uses like the internet to find like the tower that he can vaguely remember goes back and walks his way back into the village and finds his mom. Wow. I want to watch the movie now. What's it called? Line. That was awesome. I got chills just listening to the movie. Spoilers. All right. You want to watch it? Cool. All right. So it comes back to this. It comes back here to the external internal desires of the hero, right? External desires for me and this story I shared my external desire was I want to make a bunch of money online. And so that's what I set out to be and that's what I achieved. I did it. I got a bunch of funnels to make a bunch of money online. Yay. But nobody really cares. The internal is like I want to support my wife. I wanted to have more time with my family, things like that. And that's the transformation because of this thing that was created because of this journey I went on. Now I actually can have my internal desires. Now I can spend time like kids. Now I do have money to help my wife. We can go on vacation or whatever that might be for you. Does that make sense? So that's where it's taking these initial things we brought up and that's the journey of achievement and then the journey of transformation. Back to the heroes. Cool.
